verses.text: Moreover Ahithophel said unto Absalom, Let me now choose out twelve thousand men, and I will arise and pursue after David this night: And I will come upon him while he is weary and weak handed, and will make him afraid: and all the people that are with him shall flee; and I will smite the king only: And I will bring back all the people unto thee: the man whom thou seekest is as if all returned: so all the people shall be in peace. Guidance: Ahithophel's second counsel — the strategic masterpiece. Three precise components. (1) 'Twelve thousand men… this night.' Speed. A rapid-strike force, deployed tonight, before David can put any distance between himself and Jerusalem. The number is large enough to overwhelm David's 600 Gittites but small enough to move fast. (2) 'I will come upon him while he is weary and weak handed.' Exploit the exhaustion of the flight. David is at the Jordan crossing, his men have just walked twenty miles weeping, and the loyalists are not yet fortified. The window is hours, not days. (3) The political insight: 'I will smite the king only… and I will bring back all the people unto thee.' Ahithophel's surgical genius — target David personally, not the rebellion of all David's followers. Once David is dead, the followers will return to whoever holds the throne. 'The man whom thou seekest is as if all returned: so all the people shall be in peace.' Kill one man, end the war, restore peace. The counsel is, on every measure, brilliant: minimal force, minimal casualties, maximum effect, immediate execution. If it were followed, David would be dead by dawn. The chapter is showing us why Ahithophel's reputation was 'as if a man had inquired at the oracle of God' (16:23). His strategic mind is operating at peak. The chapter is also setting up the LORD's intervention. Ahithophel's counsel is so good that only a divine reversal can stop it. The reversal is going to come through Hushai's rhetorical skill, but the chapter will name the deeper cause.

And the saying pleased Absalom well, and all the elders of Israel. Guidance: Initial consensus. Absalom and the rebellion's senior leadership — the elders of Israel — find Ahithophel's plan persuasive. The verse is critical for what follows. If Absalom had acted on this initial approval, he would have won the war that night. He does not. The next sentence introduces the question that will undo him: 'Call now Hushai.' The chapter is showing us that the rebellion almost won, and that one consultation away from victory is sometimes the consultation that loses everything. There is a category of overconfidence that, having a perfect plan, still seeks a second opinion and accepts the worse one. Absalom is the type. The LORD has built the Davidic counter-strike on Absalom's habit of soliciting more opinions than he needs.

Then said Absalom, Call now Hushai the Archite also, and let us hear likewise what he saith. And when Hushai was come to Absalom, Absalom spake unto him, saying, Ahithophel hath spoken after this manner: shall we do after his saying? if not; speak thou. Guidance: Absalom summons Hushai. Why? Possibly because he genuinely values diverse counsel; possibly because Ahithophel and Hushai are court rivals and consulting both is a habit; possibly because the LORD is moving Absalom's heart toward asking the question that will doom him. The narrator does not analyze the motive. He just records the request. Absalom briefs Hushai on Ahithophel's plan and asks plainly: 'Shall we do after his saying? if not, speak thou.' The invitation is straightforward. Hushai now has the floor. He has been waiting for exactly this moment since David sent him back in 15:34. He is not going to attack Ahithophel's plan directly — that would seem disloyal to the king's most senior counselor. He is going to attack it on terrain Absalom can be made to feel personally — by stoking Absalom's fear of David's military skill and Absalom's desire for personal glory in a national campaign. The chapter is showing us the rhetoric of a man with a single chance to alter history.

Then said Absalom, Call now Hushai the Archite also, and let us hear likewise what he saith. And when Hushai was come to Absalom, Absalom spake unto him, saying, Ahithophel hath spoken after this manner: shall we do after his saying? if not; speak thou. Guidance: Absalom summons Hushai. Why? The narrator does not analyze the motive. He just records the request. Absalom briefs Hushai on Ahithophel's plan and asks plainly: 'Shall we do after his saying? if not, speak thou.' The invitation is straightforward. Hushai now has the floor. He has been waiting for exactly this moment since David sent him back in 15:34. He is not going to attack Ahithophel's plan directly — that would seem disloyal to the king's most senior counselor. And Hushai said unto Absalom, The counsel that Ahithophel hath given is not good at this time. For, said Hushai, thou knowest thy father and his men, that they be mighty men, and they be chafed in their minds, as a bear robbed of her whelps in the field: and thy father is a man of war, and will not lodge with the people. Behold, he is hid now in some pit, or in some other place: and it will come to pass, when some of them be overthrown at the first, that whosoever heareth it will say, There is a slaughter among the people that follow Absalom. And he also that is valiant, whose heart is as the heart of a lion, shall utterly melt: for all Israel knoweth that thy father is a mighty man, and they which be with him are valiant men. Guidance: Hushai's argument is constructed in three stages. (1) Concede the timing: 'Not good at this time.' Not 'wrong' (a direct attack on Ahithophel would be impolitic); just 'not at this time.' (2) Build the threat-picture of David. 'Mighty men… chafed in their minds, as a bear robbed of her whelps' — David's six hundred are not weary; they are enraged. The bear-cub image is one of the most violent in Hebrew (compare Prov 17:12); a mother bear deprived of her cubs is the apex predator-in-pain. Hushai is asking Absalom to picture his father's men in that emotional state. (3) The hidden-king detail: 'Thy father is a man of war, and will not lodge with the people.' David will not sleep among his troops where Ahithophel's strike force could find him; he will be 'hid now in some pit, or in some other place.' The detail is shrewd — David is a master of evasion from his wilderness years, and Hushai is reminding Absalom that David's specialty is precisely this kind of hide-and-strike. (4) The catastrophic-news scenario: 'When some of them be overthrown at the first, that whosoever heareth it will say, There is a slaughter among the people that follow Absalom.' Hushai inverts Ahithophel's tactical confidence. What if the strike force takes early casualties? News of any reversal will be magnified into 'rebellion in collapse,' and the army's morale will dissolve. Even the most valiant — 'whose heart is as the heart of a lion' — will melt. The argument is, strategically, weaker than Ahithophel's; rapid attack is in fact the right move against a fleeing king. But the argument has been calibrated to feed Absalom's underlying fear of his father's competence.

Therefore I counsel that all Israel be generally gathered unto thee, from Dan even to Beer-sheba, as the sand that is by the sea for multitude; and that thou go to battle in thine own person. So shall we come upon him in some place where he shall be found, and we will light upon him as the dew falleth on the ground: and of him and of all the men that are with him there shall not be left so much as one. Moreover, if he be gotten into a city, then shall all Israel bring ropes to that city, and we will draw it into the river, until there be not one small stone found there. Guidance: Hushai's alternative is grandiose and inviting to Absalom's vanity. (1) 'All Israel be generally gathered unto thee, from Dan even to Beer-sheba.' Mass mobilization — the full national army, every able-bodied man across the country. The image: 'as the sand that is by the sea for multitude.' Ten times the size of Ahithophel's twelve thousand. (2) 'That thou go to battle in thine own person.' Personal generalship — Absalom himself leading the army. This is the bait. Ahithophel's plan had relegated Absalom to the throne while sending Ahithophel out with the troops; Hushai's plan puts Absalom at the head of the army where the credit for victory will be his. (3) The poetic confidence: 'We will light upon him as the dew falleth on the ground.' The image is of overwhelming, ubiquitous, inescapable encirclement — like dew, the army would be on every blade of grass. 'Of him and of all the men that are with him there shall not be left so much as one.' Total annihilation. (4) The ridiculous-image flourish: 'If he be gotten into a city, then shall all Israel bring ropes to that city, and we will draw it into the river, until there be not one small stone found there.' The hyperbole appeals to the rebel imagination — we are so many that we could pull a fortified city into the river with ropes. The argument is strategically inferior to Ahithophel's, but rhetorically irresistible to a king-in-waiting who wants the glory of leading the army that defeats his famous-warrior father. Hushai has read Absalom precisely.

And Absalom and all the men of Israel said, The counsel of Hushai the Archite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel. For the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom. Guidance: The chapter's hinge. 'Absalom and all the men of Israel said, The counsel of Hushai is better than the counsel of Ahithophel.' The unanimous decision of the rebel leadership. They have just chosen, by every measurable strategic standard, the wrong plan. Then the narrator's editorial: 'For the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom.' The LORD's hand. The Hebrew is precise: tzivvah YHWH le-hafer — 'the LORD had commanded to break.' The verb is the same root used of breaking covenants and treaties. The LORD had pre-decreed the failure of Ahithophel's counsel. David's prayer in 15:31 ('turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness') has just been answered. Hushai's persuasive rhetoric is the means; the LORD's appointment is the cause. The chapter is showing us one of the clearest pictures in scripture of how providence operates through human agency. Hushai delivered a real speech; Absalom genuinely chose; the LORD ordained the outcome. The three causes do not compete; they cooperate. The verse is also one of the soberest verses about Absalom. 'To the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom.' Absalom is going to die in chapter 18. The chapter is telling us that the strategic blunder of v11-13 is the proximate cause; the LORD's appointed judgment is the ultimate cause. There is a category of bad decision that, while looking like personal choice, is the form God's pre-announced verdict takes. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's hand can be present even in what appears to be a court vote of free agents.

Then said Hushai unto Zadok and to Abiathar the priests, Thus and thus did Ahithophel counsel Absalom and the elders of Israel; and thus and thus have I counselled. Now therefore send quickly, and tell David, saying, Lodge not this night in the plains of the wilderness, but speedily pass over; lest the king be swallowed up, and all the people that are with him. Guidance: Hushai immediately activates the intelligence relay David set up in chapter 15. He goes to the priests Zadok and Abiathar with the full information: Ahithophel's counsel, my counsel, the decision. The urgency in his closing words: 'Lodge not this night in the plains of the wilderness, but speedily pass over.' Hushai knows that Absalom might still reconsider, or that Ahithophel might force a second hearing, or that Absalom might launch some smaller pursuit independent of the main plan. David needs to be across the Jordan tonight, not tomorrow. The chapter is showing us that even a major strategic victory at the council table does not produce safety until the threatened party is physically out of reach. The intelligence Hushai sends is not just descriptive but prescriptive — it tells David what to do. The chapter is teaching us that good intelligence work delivers both 'what is happening' and 'what therefore to do.' Hushai is a model spymaster.

Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz stayed by En-rogel; for they might not be seen to come into the city: and a wench went and told them; and they went and told king David. Nevertheless a lad saw them, and told Absalom: but they went both of them away quickly, and came to a man's house in Bahurim, which had a well in his court; whither they went down. And the woman took and spread a covering over the well's mouth, and spread ground corn thereon; and the thing was not known. And when Absalom's servants came to the woman to the house, they said, Where is Ahimaaz and Jonathan? And the woman said unto them, They be gone over the brook of water. And when they had sought and could not find them, they returned to Jerusalem. Guidance: The intelligence-relay action. Jonathan (Abiathar's son) and Ahimaaz (Zadok's son) — the two priestly-son couriers David appointed in 15:27 — are stationed at En-rogel, a spring just south of Jerusalem (the modern Bir Ayyub). They cannot enter Jerusalem without arousing suspicion, so a 'wench' (a young servant-girl, probably from one of the priests' households) carries the message to them, and they relay it to David. But a boy sees the couriers and reports to Absalom. The chapter goes into spy-thriller mode. Two royal couriers, on foot, race north toward the Jordan with the rebel king's intelligence service in pursuit. They arrive at Bahurim — the same town where Shimei cursed David in chapter 16 — and a sympathetic resident hides them in his courtyard well. (The well is a dry seasonal cistern, not a water-well.) The man's wife covers the well-mouth with a tarp and spreads ground grain on top so the cistern looks like a flat threshing-spot. Absalom's servants arrive and ask where the couriers went. 'They be gone over the brook of water' — she points them in the wrong direction. The search party leaves; the couriers climb out; the message is delivered. The chapter is one of the most realistic intelligence-relay scenes in the Old Testament — well-cover, wife's deception, false-direction, all standard espionage. The narrator is showing us that the LORD's deliverance of David has involved an extended network: priests, sons, servant-girls, sympathetic strangers, all functioning as the underground that gets information across enemy lines. The kingdom is being held together by people whose names we mostly don't know.

Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz stayed by En-rogel; for they might not be seen to come into the city: and a wench went and told them; and they went and told king David. Nevertheless a lad saw them, and told Absalom: but they went both of them away quickly, and came to a man's house in Bahurim, which had a well in his court; whither they went down. And the woman took and spread a covering over the well's mouth, and spread ground corn thereon; and the thing was not known. And when Absalom's servants came to the woman to the house, they said, Where is Ahimaaz and Jonathan? And the woman said unto them, They be gone over the brook of water. And when they had sought and could not find them, they returned to Jerusalem. Guidance: The intelligence-relay action. Jonathan (Abiathar's son) and Ahimaaz (Zadok's son) — the two priestly-son couriers David appointed in 15:27 — are stationed at En-rogel, a spring just south of Jerusalem (the modern Bir Ayyub). They cannot enter Jerusalem without arousing suspicion, so a 'wench' (a young servant-girl, probably from one of the priests' households) carries the message to them, and they relay it to David. But a boy sees the couriers and reports to Absalom. The chapter goes into spy-thriller mode. Two royal couriers, on foot, race north toward the Jordan with the rebel king's intelligence service in pursuit. They arrive at Bahurim — the same town where Shimei cursed David in chapter 16 — and a sympathetic resident hides them in his courtyard well. (The well is a dry seasonal cistern, not a water-well.) The man's wife covers the well-mouth with a tarp and spreads ground grain on top so the cistern looks like a flat threshing-spot. Absalom's servants arrive and ask where the couriers went. 'They be gone over the brook of water' — she points them in the wrong direction. The search party leaves; the couriers climb out; the message is delivered. The chapter is one of the most realistic intelligence-relay scenes in the Old Testament — well-cover, wife's deception, false-direction, all standard espionage. The narrator is showing us that the LORD's deliverance of David has involved an extended network: priests, sons, servant-girls, sympathetic strangers, all functioning as the underground that gets information across enemy lines. The kingdom is being held together by people whose names we mostly don't know.

And it came to pass, after they were departed, that they came up out of the well, and went and told king David, and said unto David, Arise, and pass quickly over the water: for thus hath Ahithophel counselled against you. Then David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan: by the morning light there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan. Guidance: The couriers reach David. They deliver Hushai's instruction: cross the Jordan tonight. David acts immediately. 'David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan: by the morning light there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan.' Every man crossed. The detail is precise: by dawn, the entire David-loyalist column is on the east side of the Jordan. The river crossing has been the strategic objective; once across, David is in trans-Jordanian territory where pursuit becomes much harder, and he is approaching Mahanaim — the fortified town where Ish-bosheth once ran his own kingdom (2:8) — which has the defenses and the loyalist Gileadite population David needs. The chapter is showing us a king saved by a single nighttime decision — and that decision triggered by a courier system, a sympathetic woman, a well, and the LORD's earlier appointment to confuse Ahithophel's counsel. There is a category of providence in which God uses fifteen different people, none of whom can see what the others are doing, to produce a single decisive crossing of a river.

And it came to pass, after they were departed, that they came up out of the well, and went and told king David, and said unto David, Arise, and pass quickly over the water: for thus hath Ahithophel counselled against you. Then David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan: by the morning light there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan. Guidance: The couriers reach David. They deliver Hushai's instruction: cross the Jordan tonight. David acts immediately. 'David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan: by the morning light there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan.' Every man crossed. The detail is precise: by dawn, the entire David-loyalist column is on the east side of the Jordan. The river crossing has been the strategic objective; once across, David is in trans-Jordanian territory where pursuit becomes much harder, and he is approaching Mahanaim — the fortified town where Ish-bosheth once ran his own kingdom (2:8) — which has the defenses and the loyalist Gileadite population David needs. The chapter is showing us a king saved by a single nighttime decision — and that decision triggered by a courier system, a sympathetic woman, a well, and the LORD's earlier appointment to confuse Ahithophel's counsel. There is a category of providence in which God uses fifteen different people, none of whom can see what the others are doing, to produce a single decisive crossing of a river.

And when Ahithophel saw that his counsel was not followed, he saddled his ass, and arose, and gat him home to his house, to his city, and put his household in order, and hanged himself, and died, and was buried in the sepulchre of his father. Guidance: Ahithophel takes his own life. The verbs are deliberate, methodical, awful. 'Saddled his ass, and arose, and gat him home… and put his household in order, and hanged himself.' Each step is calm, sequenced, complete. He knows the rebellion is going to fail; he reads Absalom's preference for Hushai's plan as the strategic blunder that will doom the entire operation; he calculates that David, restored to the throne, will execute Ahithophel for treason; he makes financial and inheritance arrangements for his family; and then he hangs himself before David can execute him. The 'put his household in order' detail is the tragedy's heart: Ahithophel dies a methodical, almost bureaucratic death. He goes home, sorts out his affairs, and ends his life with the same precision he brought to political strategy. He is buried with his fathers — the only ceremonial honor available to him. Ahithophel is one of two named suicides in the Old Testament (with Saul); the New Testament gives him a quiet typological role as the antecedent of Judas (who also hanged himself after betraying his Lord). The chapter is showing us the end of the most brilliant political mind of the period — defeated, ironically, not by his enemies but by his own side's choice of an inferior counselor. The LORD's appointed confusion of his counsel has run its full course. The man who could not bear to live under the new monarchy he had designed has eliminated himself from history. The chapter does not editorialize; it lets the precise verbs speak the tragedy.

Then David came to Mahanaim. And Absalom passed over Jordan, he and all the men of Israel with him. And Absalom made Amasa captain of the host instead of Joab: which Amasa was a man's son, whose name was Ithra an Israelite, that went in to Abigail the daughter of Nahash, sister to Zeruiah Joab's mother. So Israel and Absalom pitched in the land of Gilead. Guidance: The geography compresses. David reaches Mahanaim — the trans-Jordanian fortified town that becomes his base of operations. Absalom, following Hushai's slower plan, eventually crosses the Jordan with all his forces. The narrator notes Absalom's command appointment: Amasa replaces Joab as army commander. Amasa is Joab's first cousin — both the sons of women who were sisters (Abigail and Zeruiah, both daughters of Nahash; Joab is Zeruiah's son, Amasa is Abigail's son). Both are nephews of David through these sisters. The nepotism is Absalom's mistake: he is appointing a less experienced commander against the most decisive Davidic general for purely family-loyalty reasons. Absalom encamps in Gilead — the rolling country of central trans-Jordan. The two armies are now in the same theater, the battle is set up for chapter 18.

And it came to pass, when David was come to Mahanaim, that Shobi the son of Nahash of Rabbah of the children of Ammon, and Machir the son of Ammiel of Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite of Rogelim, Brought beds, and basons, and earthen vessels, and wheat, and barley, and flour, and parched corn, and beans, and lentiles, and parched pulse, And honey, and butter, and sheep, and cheese of kine, for David, and for the people that were with him, to eat: for they said, The people is hungry, and weary, and thirsty, in the wilderness. Guidance: The chapter closes with one of the most touching scenes in 2 Samuel. Three local notables show up at Mahanaim with supplies. (1) Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of Ammon — probably the brother of Hanun (whose family David honored in chapter 10); the Ammonite royal family is sending an envoy with goods to David. The kindness David showed in chapter 10 has produced a return-payment now. (2) Machir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar — the same Machir who had sheltered Mephibosheth (9:4-5) before David brought him to the king's table. The kindness shown to Mephibosheth has produced a return-payment now. (3) Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim — a major regional landowner who appears for the first time but will reappear in chapter 19 as the elderly benefactor David honors. The chapter is one of the cleanest pictures in the Old Testament of how kindness shown years earlier becomes provision needed in the moment of crisis. The inventory is exhaustive: bedding, basins, cooking vessels, wheat, barley, flour, roasted grain, beans, lentils, more roasted grain, honey, butter, sheep, cheese. The variety tells us these are three separate substantial gifts, designed to provision an entire army-in-exile for weeks. The motivation: 'the people is hungry, and weary, and thirsty, in the wilderness.' The chapter ends with David's army not just safely across the Jordan, but well-fed and properly equipped in fortified Mahanaim — ready to fight the decisive battle of chapter 18. The LORD's deliverance has used Hushai's counsel, the courier's relay, the woman's well, the LORD's appointed confusion of Ahithophel, and now three foreign-loyal patrons to set David up for the counterstroke. The chapter is a textbook in providence: God's hand operating through dozens of human agents, each of whom did one part of what they could see, none of whom understood the whole. Tomorrow, chapter 18, the rebellion will end at Mahanaim's forest.
